comments: Medwed is the best prof I have ever had at HLS, he was just visiting this year but I am hoping to see him hired at HLS permanently. He genuinely cares about students' understanding of the material.
Loved it! Medwed cares a lot about his students and works on his pedagogical method (unlike other teachers) to ensure that he is clear and impactful in every class.
Great professor! Evidence with Medwed is the right choice if you want someone approachable and engaging. Strong recaps and the 3-credit format keeps it manageable.
ev_m.note: Visiting prof from Northeastern. Students note strong recaps and accessible teaching style.

comments: Schulman is a great professor for evidence. She takes what is a very rule-based course and makes it interesting and timely. Classes seemed to go by fast every day, and she incorporates lots of videos in her presentations. Her exam was somewhat difficult (but not unexpectedly so) yet fair.

Not a morning person, but Schulman had me excited to go to class at 8 AM. She's an incredibly engaging lecturer with a great sense of humor who makes the doctrinal subject very clear. Exam is a bit of a time crunch, but not particularly difficult.
Schulman is AMAZING. Class is entertaining and she makes the material clear. She's also extremely willing to help you outside of class. Only downside is that she doesn't release old exams.
she is an INCREDIBLE professor who teaches you real, practical skills to use in a courtroom. one of the best classes I've taken in law school.
tips: Exam was around 18 short questions, going over all the important rules. Pretty difficult — exam is a race against the clock.
Remember & understand all the hearsay rules so you don't need to look at your outline. Do practice exams. H is possible but not guaranteed.
A little like a typing contest. Learn the rules well, make a great outline early, and do practice exams.

comments: Rubin is a great (and hilarious) professor! He's a judge in Massachusetts during the day and it's really interesting to hear his perspective on Evidence from the bench. Very approachable, no cold calls.
Good class, liked Rubin. He seems to evoke polarized opinions: you either love him or hate him. His style might grate some; if you like the persona he projects, it's a great and amusing class.
ev_r.note: Judge Rubin — bench perspective on evidence. Visiting/part-time. Polarizing style but very approachable.

comments: Know the exam format going in — many short answer questions, very fast-paced.
Worth ranking first. MUST rank first.
Do the practice problems in the book and attend class — the lectures are really helpful and incredibly clear. The practice problems in the supplement are great exam prep.

I don't know why you would take corporations with any other professor. Holger is the best!! The class is not stressful, he rarely cold calls, and you will leave the semester actually understanding corporate law.

I loved corporations with Spamann. Before this class, I had zero interest in corps. Now, I'm thinking of taking another Spamann class. There are few, if any, old-fashioned court cases; instead, professor explains the economic/political rationale behind corporate law and assigns HBS-like case studies.
Spamann is genius (just search how many degrees he has) and one of the most effective, thought-provoking, hilarious professors I have ever met.
tips: Multiple choice and 2 short essays (not policy heavy, more like issue spotter but not quite).
Short doctrinal questions, issue spotter-like questions that are open-ended and focus on legal/business questions the board faces.
bid: Extremely popular. Perhaps now it's easier to bid because his popularity has convinced him to teach two corporations classes each year.

comments: No cold calls and very entertaining in class. Fried is a very funny lecturer. He covers the doctrine in a clear way, and makes his own (short) readings, so the 'textbook' is free!

My friend asked about the rule of the duty of loyalty and Fried said something like: 'This is not a rule-based class. You don't need to know the rule. I want you to understand the boardroom dynamics.' If you don't want to bog down with a rule chart and are remotely interested in corporations, do it with Fried.
Wonderful professor, very clear and interesting classes. Reading helps but is not necessary. Guy is knowledgeable and hilarious. Shoots down gunners and LLMs for sport.
tips: MCs, issue spotters and a policy question. It helps to note his claims in class notes — he will ask in the final exam if you disagree with any of his claims made in class.
Short essay questions. He tends to be free market/pro-flexibility. I also made a joke or two, echoing his cynicism about corporate law — that worked.
bid: I don't recall anyone complaining about not being able to take his class.

comments: Great J-term class! Many amazing practitioners will come in to advise you on your trial work, and often they give very on-point feedback. The class is definitely intensive, but because it's not graded, the only pressure comes from you not wanting to look unprepared in front of your peers.
Echo everything above. Despite the intense schedule, I still think it's one of the best classes I took at HLS. The feedback from all the trial lawyers and judges was just invaluable.
tips: No exam. Only two mock trials, but the class is credit/fail.
bid: I bid it 4th and got in directly.

comments: Great professor! Worth taking it even if you have zero interest in practicing as a Bankruptcy lawyer. Roe is extremely organized, clear, and concise — a very rare quality at HLS, especially for transactional law professors.
I was surprised by how much I enjoyed this class and how useful it was for working in a law firm. If you do the work alongside the class, you will be in fine shape for the exam.
Cold call policy is quite random and doesn't matter much to the final grade. The course material is extremely useful as long as your future work touches upon debt.
Textbook is written by the professor. The course focuses only on Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Good class, very knowledgeable professor.
tips: Rumors say that he gives LPs, but you will have to really try to screw up. The exam is fairly standard. If you understand the code, you can get a good grade.
Multiple choice questions + issue spotter. Easy H in my opinion — if you take the time to really understand the rules and cases it's easy to stand out.
Past exams are super helpful since his exams follow pretty similar format.
bid: Popular, need to bid in the top 3 positions.
No need to rank very high — there were empty seats after the drop-add period.

comments: Best course I've taken at HLS. Fisher is an amazing lecturer, and really cares about covering the emerging doctrines and theories behind recent cases. A lot of his examples relate to artworks, music (e.g., Katy Perry, Dua Lipa), design, architecture, media trend. If you are into soft IP, this class is a cannot-miss.

Professor Fisher is uniquely effective as a professor, and his distinct teaching method should be adopted by more professors. This is a 4-credit class that only meets twice a week. For the other class, you watch Fisher's prerecorded videos on your own time — the videos are very clear on the doctrinal law.
One of my favorite classes at HLS. Fisher is an engaging lecturer who dives deeply not only into the doctrines but also into their rationales and the philosophical debates underlying IP law.
tips: In-class exam is an issue spotter — quite straightforward — so the paper might be the tie-breaker for most people.
8-hour take-home exam that includes an issue spotter and a policy essay.

comments: The exam covered a lot of the articles/non-case materials on the syllabus, so be sure to read and take notes on those!
Great class. Learned a lot. Hard grader.
Great professor!! Loved the class.
f_adm.note: Freeman (fall). Hard grader. Articles/non-case materials appear on exam.

comments: Brilliant class, and probably the best admin course at the law school. Brutal brutal brutal curve.

Administrative law is obviously a really important subject. Prof. Vermeule offers a really interesting theory of administrative law which he imbues throughout the course. He really shines at making sense of mysterious/conflicting doctrine.
Really boring class. Very theoretical and about Vermeule's ideas. Tends to be a gunner class with a lot of Law Review people. The curve is brutal.
tips: 3 hour exam with unpredictable format. Past exams may be representative, but he's changed the format over the years.
bid: Rank it 1 or 2; it's a big class but you will not get in if you rank it below 3.

comments: I got a lot out of this course, and I'm glad I took it. Block has so much real-world experience in the administrative state. Insightful about the realities of rulemaking, litigation strategy, and separation of powers.

This is probably the most chill Admin class you could hope for. Block cold calls on the facts of the case, but will let you easily pass. The real-life insights on how 'the sausage is made' are genuinely helpful.
I was very frustrated by the organization of this class. However, the real-life insights on how decisions actually get made really help if you want to understand admin law beyond the doctrine.
sp_adm_b.note: Block teaches from a regulatory policy + executive power lens. Take-home exam.

comments: This is the best class at HLS. Sachs teaches you an encyclopediac amount about conflicts and threads the theory with practical implications throughout. He is brilliant, kind, and organized. If you care at all about litigation, take this class.
If Erie and personal jurisdiction excite you, don't leave law school without taking this class.
A very challenging course, but quite possibly the most engaging course at HLS. Definitely worth taking if the prospect of a doctrine-heavy, fast-paced, practically-oriented seminar appeals to you.
tips: Grade based on papers and moot court oral exercises. No exam.

comments: Parker has a very different style — he focuses on the psychology and emotions that drive judicial decisions. Very refreshing if you're tired of doctrinal courses.
He has obnoxious views and allows people to spout their obnoxious views. Because free speech. The class discussion can be provocative.

AVOID if you want to learn First Amendment doctrine. You will barely learn any doctrine — you will however learn Richard Parker's views on the Constitution and a particular strand of critical legal theory.
sp_1a.note: Parker comes from the critical legal studies tradition; analysis is more structural than technical. No cold calls.

comments: This is the best class I took at HLS. Feldman is a showman. Every class feels like you are at a lecture that you would pay to attend outside of law school. Not super focused on doctrines (to be fair 1A is also a doctrinal mess) but there are a lot of interesting discussions about philosophy, legal history, ethics, and SCOTUS gossip.
Feldman is a brilliant lecturer. Would recommend if you're interested in 1A and/or con law in general.
Really wonderful course. Feldman is extraordinary — deep, accessible, and endlessly thought-provoking. Clear best option for 1A.
tips: 8 hour exam with a 1,450 word limit so it's a bit of a wild card.

comments: Worth taking! Especially if you like history, this is a great course and gives you a good sense of First Amendment law. You're on panel every other week and have to write a comment each time you're on panel.
Great class, loved Prof. Weinrib, learned a lot. Weinrib is a decent lecturer but tends to squeeze too much material into each class.
Interesting but dry.

I found the class to be quite interesting since MacKinnon is such an icon on this topic. The class is basically her universe of sex equality; students are not really welcomed to challenge that. There's limited space for discussion, but she seems accessible during office hours. Workload is on the heavy end given it's a 3-credit course taking half a semester.
I really enjoyed the course! Just be prepared for a course from a specific viewpoint.
Best Class Ever. Professor MacKinnon is amazing. Even if you don't do any of the readings (which you should), you learn so much just from listening to her.
tips: A two-day take home or 3 × 1,000-word papers.
bid: Initially might have a waitlist but it moves pretty fast.

f_fl.note: Saris (federal judge): writing skills focus, accessible professor. Good candidate for professional writing requirement.

comments: Best 4-unit class at HLS. Reading is about 20 casebook pages per night. Sachs is an amazing professor who genuinely cares about his students.

I LOVED this class. Sachs is a brilliant professor, and a really kind human being. It's a very 'law school' class (i.e., Socratic cold calls) but in the best possible way — he calls on people who seem engaged.
Very clear and organized. Sachs engages with issues critically and from multiple perspectives. There are also plenty of outlines that basically have everything he says in class.

I highly recommend this seminar if you're interested in the topic. Chen is extremely clear in explaining doctrine and also leads insightful discussions about activism and advocacy that apply to a wide range of social issues. You'll learn a lot more in this class than the generic 4-credit BLL classes and really engage with the material. The reading is on the high side though.
tips: You only have to do a few response papers and a slightly longer (only 1,000 word) final paper.

comments: I cannot recommend this course highly enough. The readings are scoped to only the doctrine you need, and the class is a practical masterclass in how to actually run a class action. Four writing assignments throughout the semester. Getting comments on briefs from an experienced litigator is very helpful.

The class teaches both class action doctrines and legal writing. Clary tries to assign only the relevant excerpts, so the overall amount of readings is manageable. No cold call but class participation is part of the grade.

Really enjoyed the class. An overview of complex litigation, with a focus on MDLs. Clary was the litigation chair at Cravath before he retired. He tells amazing war stories and provides practical tips. Class was well organized. No cold call. Manageable amount of reading. Cases serve only as examples, the statutes matter more.
tips: 8 hr final, 5,000 words limit. Typical issue spotters with one short policy question. Pay attention to circuit splits and the jurisdictions mentioned in the questions, you may get bonus points there.

comments: Super chill but insightful seminar on white collar crime. Professors are practitioners with lots of experience. They also bring in a lot of guest speakers to talk about their experience with various aspects of corporate crime. No exam, only a 10-page paper at the end. Highly recommend.
Start thinking about an essay topic early and some general themes in the class. Put effort into the paper, bluebook footnotes included.
tips: No exam. 10-page paper.

comments: It is very clear, interesting and comprehensive, covering three topics: copyright, trademark, and trial. Good choice if you want to know all three (or soft IP) quickly in a 2-credit course. Downside is that lectures can be packed with information, since each topic is worth a separate course.
Use the slides she provides to create your outline — the reading is helpful but Professor Cendali assigns entire cases which can be dense. She is very knowledgeable and made class very fun.
tips: Focus on the key cases — no need to look at the small cases she mentioned in one sentence or two. Kind of a typing competition.
bid: No need to rank high, still vacancies after add/drop.

comments: One of the nicest persons in the world. If you do not like answering policy questions, Brennan's taxation is your go-to course. Very detailed and well structured outlines online. He speaks very quietly so sometimes it's hard to hear/understand. But with the help of the outlines you will have no trouble mastering the subject matter.
tips: You will get a good grade if you take the time to study the rules and examples in the slides.

comments: Quite econ and finance focused. Prof is really nice and very willing to slow down to help students process. Whichever professor you choose, I strongly recommend taking taxation some time at law school. It is one of the most useful courses, no matter which area of law you are interested in.
tips: Class mirrors Warren's structure so consider his outlines as well. Policy heavy so no time pressure. Past exam questions sometimes recur.

comments: A pretty easy-to-follow class that covers a wide range of basic economic topics that relate to business management. I'd actually recommend anyone who's studied economics in college NOT to take this class — you've probably learned most of the stuff this class covers.
Very basic business and economics concepts. Problem sets required and most helpful for exam prep.

One of the best classes I've taken at HLS. Ellias's lecture has great cadence and was never boring. The course covers the basics of the bankruptcy process with focus on Chapters 3, 5, and 11 (mostly relevant to corporate restructuring). The case law demonstrates what Ellias calls 'beautiful lawyering' among various parties fighting over assets of a distressed company in a zero-sum game.
Loved it. Ellias has been my favorite professor. Excellent class — very clear in his explanations and very willing to meet with students.
tips: Pretty standard issue spotters and policy questions.
bid: Not difficult to get in — I got in from the waitlist.

comments: One of my fav courses at HLS! Super engaging and caring prof, lots of amazing guest speakers. I loved discussions of Andrew Lloyd Webber and Taylor Swift, especially.
Agree with everything above. Fun, useful and easy H.
This class is a ton of fun! Reading is usually light and involves a lot of pop cultural references. There is no exam; only two blog posts and a final paper.
tips: Paper + canvas post.

comments: Professor is very helpful. Recommend for anyone without a finance/econ background.
Helpful for anyone without an econ background, and quite easy if you have one. The format is similar to an undergrad econ class (weekly assignments + 2 exams).
tips: Lots of practice materials, do a few of them and you'll be fine.
Blog posts instead of a long final paper. Students contribute three substantive posts to the Access to Justice Lab's blog.
sp_a2j.note: Easy H. Great for those interested in access to civil justice.

comments: This was probably one of the most relevant and effectively taught courses I've ever taken at HLS. Michelman is a practitioner with real expertise — he makes the rules come alive through actual cases he and his colleagues have litigated.
If you want to go civil rights law or will be working with civil rights over summer, this class is incredibly helpful.

This was a great and very practical class. If you feel like you're going to come out of law school without knowing who your clients actually are or where the law actually is right now, take this class.
sp_crl.note: Michelman — civil rights litigation. Directly relevant to litigation track.

comments: The seminar is informal and conversational. Clinical work is fun but occasionally intense. A lot of opportunities to interact with clients. Fair amount of chances to do oral argument in court (most likely in the small claims session). Chances to write complaint, dispositive motions, and sometimes do discovery related work. The two supervisors are knowledgeable and very nice, but often hands-off, so you really have to be in charge of all your cases. Great opportunity for people interested in litigation.
tips: The seminar has no exam. One final paper and presentation, could be on anything consumer-protection related.

comments: I didn't like the cyberlaw clinic much because I didn't work well with my supervisor. However, my peers generally had a good time and Chris Bavitz is an incredible professor. The seminar is not uninteresting.

Your project and supervisor can make or break the experience. Make sure to fill out the survey as specifically as possible to get a project that you like. Relatively low stress and great learning experience.
tips: Mid-semester reflection, end-of-semester reflection, 2 blog posts and a short presentation. No exam.

comments: Highly recommend! Students apply for and complete an externship at a federal court anywhere in the country (in person during J term and remote in the spring), with HLS covering housing and travel expenses. Great way to escape Cambridge in January — I externed at SDNY and had an amazing experience drafting opinions and going to hearings/trials for many very, very interesting cases.
tips: The clinic includes a seminar in the spring, which requires two reflection papers (2 pages) and a final paper (10 pages). The professor was busy and flexible with the deadline.
bid: Very competitive. Even ranking it #1 does not guarantee a spot.

comments: Clinical placement is a judicial internship at a trial court in Boston. You can choose federal or state. I was at MA superior court with Judge Sarah Ellis. Very rewarding experience. Wrote a couple memos/draft opinions for the judge. Observed lots of cool trials/hearings. Learned a ton. Definitely one of my favorite classes at HLS. The clinical seminar invites lots of guest speakers such as judges from different courts.
tips: 3 very short (1–2 page) reflection papers + final reflection or final paper. Very easy H.

comments: Clinical placement is in one of the DA's offices around Massachusetts. Students would usually have the chance to perform arraignments, pleas, jury trials, and other tasks associated with standing for cases. They would also observe prosecutors and second-chair trials. Corrigan's seminar is super chill and he makes a lot of allowances if you're busy in court. Highly recommend if you're looking for lots of hands-on trial experience.
bid: I bid #1, but it's probably not too competitive.

comments: Highly recommend. It's like a relaxed version of TAW. The course is organized as a mock trial. You will go through the whole process of deposition, hearing, motion, cross/direct examination, oral argument etc. You will be arguing in front of real Fed Cir judges and there will be a jury. I learned tons of practical stuff.
tips: No exam. The grade is based on your writing and mock trial performance.
bid: I ranked it #5, then waited a while to get off the wl.

comments: One of the best classes I took at HLS, hands down. The material is challenging, but Professor Goldsmith makes it as clear as it can be. He is incredibly clear and creates thorough slides, which he posts. Not easy to get an H.

Fantastic class. Goldsmith teaches very clearly and effectively. Very thorough analysis of SCOTUS cases. It's like a combination of Con law and advanced Civ Pro. He assigns a lot of readings and expects everybody to work on it. He calls everyone 1–2 times per semester with 1 free pass.
Tough class but very well-taught. I probably learned more there than I did anywhere else at HLS. 'Do not confuse the necessary with the familiar.'
tips: 4hr in-class exam with issue-spotters. Not easy to get an H.
3.5 hour exam, very tough — borderline demoralizing.
bid: I ranked it first and was initially #40 on the waitlist. Others who ranked it first were #70, for context.

comments: Great class! Professor doesn't cold call so there's no real need to do the readings. I learned a lot about the constitutional law in various countries. The breadth was also wide, covering topics that's not as ostensibly important in American law, but may be consequential — like how democracies die.
tips: All MC, take good notes in class and compare notes with friends.

comments: Highly recommend this course. McCraw was so engaging in lecture, and wove stories from the NYT into his instruction. He brings in lots of amazing guest speakers including Pulitzer winners.

McCraw is very entertaining and quite organized, so this class was a pleasure to take. Would definitely recommend. Great introduction to freedom of expression through real cases — prior restraint, national security, defamation, press access.

Professor is VP and deputy GC at NYT. Great war stories, fascinating guest speakers, nice introduction to the freedom of expression with many real life examples. However, readings are long and not well excerpted.
tips: 3 hr issue spotter + short answers + 1 policy essay, no word limit. He discloses the topic of the policy question in advance, so think it through and be prepared. Sort of an easy H.

comments: Recommended even if you are not interested in the fashion industry (like me). A good introduction course with concentrated topics, including sustainability, IP rights, and ethics.
Super chill winter term, very up my alley in terms of content. Focuses on IP mostly. Jam packed with amazing guest speakers, including celebrities.
tips: Two blogs + ~15 page paper. High chance of H.

comments: Excellent professor. Very clear about course material, interesting and engaging in class, and makes a confusing field of law easy to understand for beginners in antitrust with no economics background. No cold calls. He calls on volunteers. Also only 3 credits.
tips: Three hour closed book exam. Competitive but manageable.

comments: I would recommend: each class's structure and connections aren't always super clear, so it's helpful to find another professor's outline or supplement to organize the structure. Be conscious of keeping the statutory language in front of you.
Churchill was a wonderful teacher, a fantastic in-class experience. Sometimes how he taught things were difficult to track, but the discussions were engaging.
Fantastic class. Very detailed and engaging.
tips: 8-hour take-home. Follows a somewhat consistent pattern (issue spotter + short answers + essay + book review). Not an 'easy H.'

comments: You will be working in Mass AG and will be assigned to a department of your interests. I was in the trial department and liked it a lot: mostly defending the state government in tort and civil rights cases. Others have been assigned to constitutional litigation / criminal law / antitrust, etc. Workload is light but you need to commute to the office.
